description:
You are an IT-support field technician who has just received a data dump of help-desk tickets.  
Your supervisor wants two things:

1. Confirm that the JSON data follows the company’s ticket schema.  
2. Produce a filtered list that shows only the currently open tickets handled by the “network” team.

Everything you need is already in your home directory:

• /home/user/tickets.json ‑ the raw ticket backlog.  
• /home/user/ticket_schema.json ‑ the JSON-Schema definition the file must pass.

Your job (you may use any tool available on the system, e.g. jq or a python one-liner) is to:

A. Validate /home/user/tickets.json against /home/user/ticket_schema.json.  
   • Create the file /home/user/validation.log (plain UTF-8 text).  
   • Write exactly one of the following single-line messages to it:  
       “Schema validation: PASS”  – if the file conforms  
       “Schema validation: FAIL”  – if it does not  
   (No leading/trailing spaces, keep upper-case PASS/FAIL.)

B. If validation passes, extract only the tickets that satisfy BOTH of these conditions:  
      "status" is the string “open”  
      "team"   is the string “network”  
   • Save the result to /home/user/open_network_tickets.json  
   • The output file must be a prettified JSON array (indent = 2 spaces, newline at the end).  
     Example formatting:  
       [  
         {  
           "id": 1234,  
           "title": "Something",  
           "status": "open",  
           "team": "network"  
         }  
       ]

C. Append a second line to /home/user/validation.log in the exact form:  
       Open network tickets count: N  
   where N is the number of objects you wrote to open_network_tickets.json.  
   (There must now be exactly two lines in validation.log, the first being the PASS/FAIL line, the second being the count line.)

Expected artefacts the grading system will check:

1. /home/user/validation.log  
   • line 1  – strictly “Schema validation: PASS” or “Schema validation: FAIL”  
   • line 2  – strictly “Open network tickets count: <integer>”

2. /home/user/open_network_tickets.json  
   • Valid JSON, prettified with 2-space indentation, newline-terminated.  
   • Only tickets where status == "open" and team == "network".

Use as few commands as you like (it’s fine if you solve everything in 2–3 shell commands), but make sure the two files above are created with the exact content and formatting described; the autograder is whitespace-sensitive.
